    Sprint 2 – Agents & Agentic Systems

    📄

    Reflection & Agent evaluation Frameworks

    Lesson 5

    Building production-grade AI agents is more than just getting them to perform tasks - it is about ensuring they do that reliably and correctly. One of the biggest challenges in deploying AI agents is maintaining output quality over time. In this lesson, we focus on three advanced techniques for robust AI agent development: reflection loops (agents critiquing and improving their own outputs), systematic hallucination debugging (identifying and fixing when the agent makes up incorrect information), and agent evaluation frameworks (tools like LangSmith for monitoring and benchmarking agent performance).



    Agent Reflection Loops: Improving Output Quality


    Reflection loops are an agent prompting strategy where the AI temporarily steps back to reflect on and critique its own outputs, often before finalizing a response. Instead of responding instinctively with a single pass, the agent can think twice - evaluating its initial answer and refining it. In practice, reflection trades extra computation time for a potentially large gain in output quality and accuracy. For production scenarios where quality matters more than a few seconds, this trade-off is well worth it. Below are outlined several major styles of reflection loops and how to implement them in an agent. Each style adds a layer of self-checking or self-improvement that can help catch mistakes (especially hallucinations or reasoning errors) before an answer is returned to the user.



    Simple Self-Critique Loops


    The most straightforward reflection approach is a self-critique loop. In this pattern, after the agent produces an initial answer, it evaluates its own output for errors or rule violations, usually by appending a prompt like “Check if the above answer is correct and follows the instructions.” The same LLM (or a separate instance of it) then returns a critique or a simple judgment. This critique can be as minimal as a yes/no flag or as detailed as a list of issues found. The key is that the model is judging its own response, essentially acting as an internal reviewer. This self-critique doesn’t automatically change the answer but it provides a chance to catch obvious issues. 




    Implementing Self-Critique: Implementation can be as simple as an extra prompt to the model. For instance, you might have a two-step agent: one call generates an answer, and a second call to the LLM (with a prompt like “Evaluate the above answer for correctness and any mistakes”) generates a critique. If the critique indicates problems, your system could log them or even present them to a human reviewer. In LangGraph, you can implement this as a small chain where the output of the generator node is fed into a critique node. However, by itself, a self-critique loop doesn’t change the answer - it’s just reflection. The next step is using that reflection to actually improve the output.



    Iterative Reflection with Reruns (Critique and Retry)


    A more powerful pattern is to not only critique, but also iterate based on the critique. In this reflection loop, the agent uses the self-critique feedback to attempt a revised answer. This often involves a cycle: Generate -> Reflect -> Refine, potentially repeating multiple times. The agent essentially asks itself: “How good was my answer and how can I make it better?” If issues are found, the agent tries again, using the critique as guidance. One way to implement critique-and-retry is to include the critique in the prompt for the next answer. For example, after getting a critique, you could prompt the model: “Given the above feedback, provide a new and improved answer”. The loop may be terminated either after a set number of tries or when the critique indicates success. Importantly, each iteration gives the model a fresh chance, often leading to a better thought-out solution on subsequent tries.



    Below is a simplified example of setting up a reflection loop using LangGraph. We define two nodes in a graph - a generate node for the initial answer and a reflect node for critique - and connect them in a loop. The should_continue function decides whether to loop again or end after a certain number of messages (to avoid infinite loops).


    from langgraph.graph import MessageGraph, END

builder = MessageGraph()
builder.add_node("generate", generator_node)    # Node that generates an answer
builder.add_node("reflect", reflection_node)    # Node that critiques the answer
builder.set_entry_point("generate")             # Start at the generation step

# Conditional: after generation, go to reflection (unless stopping condition met)
def should_continue(state):
    # Stop if the conversation has grown too long (prevent endless loop)
    if len(state) > 6: 
        return END
    return "reflect"

builder.add_conditional_edges("generate", should_continue)
builder.add_edge("reflect", "generate")         # After reflection, go back to generation
graph = builder.compile()


    In the above, generator_node might be a call to an LLM with the user’s prompt (producing an answer), and reflection_node could be another LLM call that takes the conversation so far and produces a critique (perhaps prompted to act like a strict reviewer). The graph will execute as: generate -> reflect -> generate -> … until should_continue returns END. Each cycle, the generator can incorporate the latest critique (since the critique is appended to the message list state) and attempt to fix any issues. This “retry with feedback” loop often produces outputs that are more accurate and aligned with instructions. I.e. the agent is using its own feedback as a guide to converge toward a better answer.


    Extended Reflection - “Reflexion” with External Feedback: A once famous variant of the reflection loop is the Reflexion strategy. Reflexion extends the basic loop by incorporating external knowledge or tool use during reflection. For example, the agent might critique its answer and realize it needs more information, then issue a search query (tool call) and use the results to improve its next answer. By forcing the reflection to be grounded in external data (e.g. retrieved facts with citations), this method steers the agent to correct factual errors more constructively. While Reflexion still loops a fixed number of times, it produces a final answer that’s better supported by evidence. This highlights that reflection can be augmented with tools and memory, the critique isn’t just free-form thoughts, but can include checking a knowledge base or performing calculations to verify the initial answer.



    The Critique-Then-Revise Pattern


    Another major style of reflection is the critique-then-revise pattern. Instead of an open-ended loop, this approach structures the process into a single pass with distinct phases: first the model drafts an answer, then it critiques that draft, and finally it revises the answer having this critique in mind. The difference from the iterative loop above is that critique-then-revise usually happens in one structured prompt or a predetermined sequence, often producing the final answer in one go (after the critique). This pattern is also similar to asking the LLM to explain it’s reasoning.


    In practice, critique-then-revise can be implemented by dividing the prompt or conversation into steps. For example, you might instruct the model with a system or few-shot prompt: Step 1 - Draft: answer the user’s question. Step 2 - Critique: identify any factual inaccuracies or weak points in the draft. Step 3 - Revise: produce a new answer that fixes the issues found, and output only the revised answer. The model will internally generate a draft, then some critique (often as a list of bullet points of errors or omissions), and finally a corrected answer. 


    This structured self-revision pattern has a few advantages:


    • Targeted improvements: By explicitly listing flaws, the model is less likely to repeat or overlook them in the revision. 

    • Reduced overconfidence: Forcing the model to label its own errors tends to lessen the false sense of certainty that leads to hallucinations.

    • No external feedback needed: The entire process uses the model itself (no human required), which means it can run autonomously within a single model API call or chain.


    However, there are some considerations:


    • Longer prompts and higher cost: Because the model is effectively doing a multi-step reasoning internally, the token usage roughly doubles (or more if the critique is long). This can increase latency and API cost. It’s a known trade-off: two passes for a better answer.

    • Knowledge limits: If the model’s knowledge is insufficient or it lacks facts, a self-critique won’t magically fix that. The model might correctly note “I do not have information about X,” but without retrieval it cannot produce the correct info out of thin air. In other words, self-critique can identify gaps, but it can’t fill knowledge gaps by itself. In such cases, pairing the critique-then-revise pattern with a retrieval step or tool use is necessary.


    This pattern is essentially an internal chain-of-thought that remains hidden from the end-user, ensuring they only see the polished result. It’s very effective for Q&A agents, explanation-generation, and even coding tasks where the first attempt might have errors that the model can catch on reflection.



    Debugging Hallucinations and Ensuring Reliability


    Hallucinations are outputs that are factually incorrect or nonsensical, but are asserted confidently by the model. It is one of the biggest problems in many LLM-based agents. Previously, we have seen how reflection loops help mitigate hallucinations by encouraging the agent to double-check itself. In this section, we focus on systematically detecting, diagnosing, and reducing hallucinations using both internal agent strategies and external tools. Debugging hallucinations is a crucial part of agent development, because even a well-designed agent will occasionally output false information (due to how LLMs work), and catching those failures is key to building trustable systems.



    What Are Hallucinations and Why Do They Happen?


    A quick recap: an LLM hallucinates when it produces content that is not grounded in reality or the provided data. For example, if asked about a document it wasn’t actually provided via the context, it might make up a plausible-sounding summary. Hallucinations are caused by the model’s training on patterns of language rather than a guaranteed source of truth - the model will fill in gaps with statistically likely text, which can easily be incorrect or made up. Common causes of hallucinations include insufficient or outdated training data, unclear prompts or goals (leading the model to guess), or constraints like limited context window causing the model to lose attention to crucial facts. In short, if an agent isn’t certain about the answer, it won’t admit by default - it will attempt to answer anyway, which may be wrong.


    In production, hallucinations are more than just wrong answers, they can damage user trust or even cause harm if the information is critical. That is why hallucination debugging is a top priority of LLMOps. We need strategies to detect them when they occur and reduce their frequency overall.



    Detecting Hallucinations


    The first step is detection: how can we tell when an agent’s output is hallucinated? This can be tricky, especially in open-ended scenarios where we don’t have a ground-truth answer readily available. We covered some types of evals in RAG evaluation already, this section will be a short refresher on the concepts:


    • Reference-Based Evaluation: If you do have a ground truth or expected answer (for example, in a QA test set or a known task), you can compare the agent’s answer to the reference. Traditional metrics like BLEU or ROGUE measure similarity to a reference answer. More specialized metrics like Answer Correctness (from RAGAS) compute factual precision and recall by breaking down the answer into facts and checking each against the reference.These methods are great for evaluation when references exist, but they assume you know the correct answer in advance - often not the case in live deployments.

    • Reference-Free Evaluation: In many agent applications, instead of a known answer, you might have a context or retrieved documents the agent is supposed to use. In that case, you can evaluate faithfulness to the provided context. One technique is to have another model (or a routine) break the agent’s answer into factual statements and verify each against the source context. If the answer contains information not present or not inferable from the context, it’s likely hallucinating. Metrics like faithfulness score how well the answer sticks to the given context. Another metric, context utilization, checks whether the agent actually used the top relevant documents, measuring if it ignored important context. In summary, if your agent uses a RAG setup, you can catch hallucinations by verifying that the answer’s claims appear in the retrieved documents. If the agent says something that you cannot find in those docs, that’s a red flag.

    • LLM-Based Fact Checking: Even without references or context, you can leverage another LLM (or even the same one) to judge the answer’s correctness. This is essentially what our reflection loops do internally. For external detection, you can use a separate Judge model that is potentially more reliable or specialized. For example, you could prompt a second model: “Here is the assistant’s answer and the user’s question. Is the answer correct and truthful? If not, point out what’s wrong”. If using the same model as both answerer and judge (self-critique), you are more likely to get false negatives (the model might miss its own mistakes) or false positives (over-correcting something that was fine), but it is still a useful signal. The judge model essentially compares the output to what it knows or to consistency standards. 

    • Human Feedback: The ultimate gold standard for detecting hallucinations is human review. Users might report “This answer seems wrong”, or internal testers might label outputs as hallucinated. While not scalable for every response, human feedback is crucial for discovering subtle or high-impact hallucinations that automated methods miss. Many systems have a feedback button or collect ratings for this reason. Additionally, for ongoing quality monitoring, human evaluation on a sample of outputs can give a more nuanced view of how often the agent hallucinates and in what ways.

    • Observability Tools & Heuristics: Sometimes, you can detect a likely hallucination by looking at the agent’s behavior. For instance, if the agent’s trace (as captured by instrumented code) shows that a tool returned no results or an error, yet the agent produced a confident answer, that answer is a suspect. Observability tools let you set up heuristics or alerts - e.g. if the agent ever says something like “According to [some source] ...” but we see no such source was actually provided, that’s a hallucination. In short, by monitoring the agent’s internal state, you can sometimes detect hallucinations in real-time and intercept them.


    In practice, production systems use a combination of these methods. For example, you might run an LLM-based evaluator on each answer and have user feedback, and perhaps daily offline evaluations against a test set with known answers.



    Root Cause Analysis and State Inspection


    Once you have detected a hallucination, the next step is to diagnose why it happened. This is where your extensive logging and state tracking pays off. Here are steps and tips for hallucination root cause analysis:

    • Inspect the prompt and conversation: Often a hallucination can be traced to how the prompt was phrased or how the conversation history is structured. Check if the user’s question was ambiguous or lacked necessary context - the agent might have guessed to fill in missing info. Or see if the system instructions were too soft (maybe you allowed the agent to make up an answer if unsure). If reflection was enabled, look at the reflection output - did the agent realize it might be wrong and then ignore that? Or did the reflection completely miss the false statement?

    • Check tool usage and knowledge retrieval: Many hallucinations in agent systems occur when the agent should have used a tool or knowledge source but either didn’t use it correctly or the tool failed silently. For example, suppose the agent was expected to call a retrieval tool to query your vector database. The logs show it did call the tool, but got no relevant results (maybe the query was poorly formed). A robust agent should then either reformulate the query or say “I could not find any info”, but a less robust one might hallucinate an answer. If your trace shows retrieval_tool -> "No results found" followed by the agent saying “According to [some book] the answer is [answer]” you have found the failure. The fix might be to adjust the agent’s logic to handle empty tool results.

    • Examine the chain-of-thought (if available): Some agent implementations allow you to see the model’s intermediate reasoning steps (the thoughts it had before taking actions). These can be very informative. For instance, the chain-of-thought might show the model made an incorrect assumption early on, and then everything snowballed. Or you might see that the model knew it didn’t have the info. If the chain-of-thought reveals such hints, you can address them: e.g. explicitly instruct the model not to fill in unknown facts.

    • Look for patterns across multiple incidents: If you have logs of many runs, try to identify if hallucinations follow a pattern. For example, you might find the agent hallucinates citations in answers because you encouraged it to provide sources but didn’t give it a tool to fetch real ones - so it just invents some. The solution would be to integrate a proper citation retrieval step or remove the requirement for sources unless they can be grounded. Tracking state like the content of memory or variables at each step can help here. If the agent carries some state, check if that state contained a mistaken fact that later became a hallucinated detail. That could mean your memory summarization needs improvement to not introduce errors.

    • Leverage Observability Dashboards: Tools like LangSmith allow you to create dashboards and alerts for certain events. For example, you could track a metric Hallucination Detected (incremented whenever your LLM-as-judge or reflection step flags something) and see how often it happens over time. If you push a new version of the agent and suddenly that metric spikes, you know something in the change caused more hallucinations. In essence, treat hallucinations as you would treat exceptions in a traditional application - monitor them, log them with detail, and investigate significant ones to fix the underlying issue.



    Reducing and Preventing Hallucinations


    After detection and diagnosis, the final goal is mitigation - make changes so that future outputs have fewer hallucinations. Some effective strategies include:


    • Prompt Engineering & Instruction Tuning: Sometimes the fix is simply to better instruct the model not to guess. A very direct prompt instruction like “If you are not sure or the information is not available, do not fabricate an answer - instead respond with an apology or a request for clarification”, can help a lot. If using few-shot examples, include a demonstration of refraining from answering when information is missing. Properly tuned system messages can make the model hallucinate less. Additionally, Chain-of-Thought prompting (“Let’s think step by step”) can reduce hallucinations in reasoning tasks by making the model break down the problem.

    • Retrieval Augmentation: As noted, a common cause of hallucination is lack of knowledge. Integrating a knowledge retrieval step (e.g., a vector database lookup or web search) can turn a question the model would have hallucinated on into one it can answer correctly. The key is ensuring the agent actually uses the retrieved info.

    • Implementing Post-Output Checks: We mentioned intercepting hallucinations after a draft answer is produced, but before it reaches the user. This can be formalized: for example, always run a “hallucination check” (could be a second LLM or a rule-based check) on the final answer. If it flags a likely incorrect claim, instead of showing that answer to the user, you could either have the agent attempt a correction, or return a fallback response. This approach effectively turns detection into prevention.

    • Tool/Reflection Improvements: If your analysis showed the agent skipped using a tool or misused it, modify the agent’s logic or prompt to emphasize the correct behavior. E.g. “If the question is about the items we have in stock, first use the retrieval tool to find information, then answer”.

    • External Knowledge Constraints: Another approach is to force the agent to show its sources (like asking it to output citations or evidence). This doesn’t eliminate hallucinations, but it makes them obvious (a citation that doesn’t actually support the claim is easy to flag). Some systems can require agents to justify answers, making it easier to audit correctness. If the agent cannot find a source, that’s a clue it might be hallucinating. This ties back into evaluation - you can score an answer higher if all its claims are backed by provided sources.

    • Continuous Learning: Finally, reducing hallucinations can be an iterative learning process. If you have a logging and evaluation setup, you can use accumulated data of where the agent made mistakes to retrain or fine-tune the model. For example, compile a dataset of (prompt, agent answer, corrected answer) from real conversations and fine-tune the model to prefer the corrected answers. Or use reinforcement learning where hallucination avoidance is part of the reward signal. This is a longer-term strategy and can be complex, personally I have not seen this done in practice.


    The main takeaway is that hallucination debugging isn't a one time process. It’s an ongoing cycle - detect issues, diagnose causes via traces and reflection, apply fixes, and then evaluate to see if the fixes worked (and didn’t introduce new issues).





    Key Components of Agent Evaluation


    • Test Datasets: Collect examples of tasks or user queries for the agent, with either known correct answers or at least some expectation of what a good response looks like. For instance, a set of factual questions with ground-truth answers, or a set of customer requests where you know how a perfect agent should respond. In LangSmith, you can create datasets and even label them with expected outputs. This serves as your benchmark suite.

    • Metrics and Criteria: Decide what aspects of the agent you want to measure. Common metrics include:

      • Correctness / Accuracy: Did the agent get the right answer?

      • Relevance: Is the answer on-topic and addressing the user’s request?

      • Hallucination Rate: Does the answer contain any unverifiable or made-up information?

      • Tool usage correctness: Did the agent use the tools as expected? For agents with tool support, you may evaluate if the sequence of tool calls (the “trajectory”) was as it should be for the task. For example, if a question required a database lookup, did the agent actually call the database tool?

      • Robustness: Does slight rephrasing of the question change the answer significantly?

      • Harmfulness / Policy adherence: Especially for public-facing agents, you might evaluate whether any responses are toxic or break guidelines.

      • Efficiency: How many steps or how much time does the agent take?

    • Automated Evaluators: For each metric, if possible, implement an automated evaluator. For example, LLM-as-judge evaluators where you provide a prompt to another model to grade the agent’s output Automated evaluators can also be simple Python functions - e.g. comparing if a tool call sequence exactly matches an expected sequence (useful in deterministic environments). The idea is to offload as much evaluation as possible to machines, so you can scale to evaluating hundreds or thousands of examples quickly.

    • Human Evaluation: Some aspects may still require human judgment. For instance, evaluating the style or helpfulness of a response might need a human rating (even though evaluator models can be fine tuned to perform the rating as well). Human eval might be done on a sample - say 50 random outputs per week - just to get a qualitative sense of any issues that automated metrics don’t catch.

    • Reporting and Benchmarking: Once you have metrics coming from automated evaluators (and possibly human ones), the framework should aggregate results and show trends. It can also break it down by category (maybe your dataset is tagged by query type or difficulty). This makes evaluation continuous - every time you update the agent or model, you run the evals and see if things improved or regressed.



    Combining Internal and External evaluation


    Finally, let’s reflect on how internal (reflection loop) and external (evaluation framework) strategies come together. They are complementary:


    • Internal reflection loops improve each individual answer in real-time. They help the agent catch its mistakes in the moment, potentially preventing a bad response from ever being seen by the user.

    • External evaluation looks at the big picture and longer term. It catches patterns of failures that may not be fully solved by one-off reflection. For example, the agent might successfully self-correct 70% of its mistakes via reflection, but the other 30% still get through - those will show up in eval metrics and logs. With that data, you might decide to fine-tune the model or add a new tool to target the remaining gaps.


    In a robust deployment, you use both. You might even integrate them - the outputs of the internal reflection (like the content of critiques) can be logged and later analyzed via your evaluation pipeline. Let’s say you notice from logs that whenever the reflection says “I don’t have enough information”, the final answer is usually wrong - that might lead you to integrate a better retrieval step whenever that occurs. This is an example of using external analysis to inform internal loop design. In short, internal loops reduce immediate errors, external frameworks guide systemic improvements, together they form a continuous improvement cycle.
